agno3.name: Silver nitrate
agno3.appearance: Colorless crystals that darken when exposed to light
agno3.storage: Store in a dark place, in sealed containers, away from organic substances
agno3.industrial: Production of photographic materials, mirrors, and antiseptics
fun_facts: Lunar caustic (silver nitrate) is used in medicine for cauterizing wounds
Leaves permanent black stains on skin due to reduction to metallic silver

al2_so4_3.name: Aluminium sulfate
al2_so4_3.industrial: Water purification (coagulant), paper manufacturing, leather tanning
al2_so4_3.production: Dissolving Al(OH)₃ or Al₂O₃ in sulfuric acid
fun_facts: One of the most widely used coagulants for drinking water purification
Undergoes significant hydrolysis when dissolved in water, creating an acidic solution

al2o3.name: Aluminium oxide
al2o3.production: Processing of bauxite using the Bayer process
fun_facts: Corundum, ruby, and sapphire are natural forms of Al₂O₃, colored by impurities
One of the hardest oxides — 9 on the Mohs scale

al_oh_3.name: Aluminium hydroxide
al_oh_3.production: Precipitation from aluminium salt solutions using alkali
fun_facts: Found in many antacid medications (heartburn remedies)
Decomposes into Al₂O₃ and water when heated

alcl3.name: Aluminium chloride
alcl3.production: Reaction of aluminium with chlorine or HCl
fun_facts: Anhydrous AlCl₃ is a key catalyst in organic synthesis (Friedel-Crafts reaction)
Its boiling point is lower than its melting point — it sublimes at atmospheric pressure

bacl2.name: Barium chloride
bacl2.storage: Store in sealed containers in a ventilated area
bacl2.industrial: Reagent for detecting sulfate ions in analytical chemistry
bacl2.production: Reaction of barium carbonate with hydrochloric acid
fun_facts: A qualitative reagent for sulfate ions — produces a white precipitate of BaSO₄

baso4.name: Barium sulfate
baso4.industrial: Radiocontrast agent, filler in paper and plastics manufacturing
baso4.production: Precipitation from barium salt solutions using sulfuric acid or sulfates
fun_facts: Used as a 'barium meal' for stomach X-rays — insoluble and non-toxic
The mineral barite (BaSO₄) is the main industrial source of barium

c2h2.name: Acetylene
c2h2.appearance: Colorless gas (technical grade has an unpleasant odor due to impurities)
c2h2.industrial: Gas welding and metal cutting, PVC production, synthetic rubber manufacturing
c2h2.production: Carbide method (CaC₂ + H₂O), thermal cracking of methane
fun_facts: An acetylene torch reaches temperatures up to 3150°C
Pure acetylene is nearly odorless — the unpleasant smell comes from phosphine and hydrogen sulfide impurities

c2h4.name: Ethylene
c2h4.appearance: Colorless gas with a faint sweet odor
c2h4.industrial: Production of polyethylene, ethanol, ethylene oxide, styrene
c2h4.production: Thermal cracking of petroleum, dehydration of ethanol
fun_facts: The most produced organic compound in the world (over 150 million tonnes/year)
A phytohormone — it accelerates fruit ripening

c2h5oh.name: Ethanol
c2h5oh.appearance: Colorless volatile liquid with a characteristic odor
c2h5oh.industrial: Solvent, fuel, antiseptic, base for alcoholic beverages
c2h5oh.production: Fermentation of sugars or hydration of ethylene
fun_facts: One of the first organic compounds known to humanity
Absolute ethanol (99.95%) is obtained by azeotropic distillation with benzene

c2h6.name: Ethane
c2h6.production: Component of natural gas (5–10%), associated petroleum gas
fun_facts: Ethane is the second simplest alkane after methane
Lakes of liquid ethane and methane exist on Saturn's moon Titan

c4h10.name: Butane
c4h10.industrial: Fuel (bottled household gas), butadiene production, solvent
c4h10.production: Processing of natural gas and petroleum
fun_facts: The main component of lighter gas
Boils at -0.5°C — almost at 0°C

c4h6.name: 1,3-Butadiene
c4h6.appearance: Colorless gas with a faint odor
c4h6.industrial: Production of synthetic rubber (polybutadiene, styrene-butadiene rubber)
c4h6.production: Dehydrogenation of butane, byproduct of cracking
fun_facts: The basis of synthetic rubber — invented by S. V. Lebedev (1910)
Conjugated double bonds give the molecule exceptional stability

c4h8.name: 1-Butene
c4h8.industrial: Production of polybutylene, butadiene, gasoline component
fun_facts: Exists as several isomers: 1-butene, 2-butene (cis- and trans-)
Used to increase the octane rating of gasoline

c5h10.name: 1-Pentene
fun_facts: 1-Pentene is one of the first liquid alkenes (boils at 30°C)

c6h12o6.name: Glucose
c6h12o6.appearance: White crystalline powder with a sweet taste
c6h12o6.production: Hydrolysis of starch or cellulose (acid or enzymatic)
fun_facts: The primary energy source for cells in the body
The silver mirror reaction is a qualitative test for the aldehyde group of glucose

c6h5cooh.name: Benzoic acid
c6h5cooh.production: Oxidation of toluene, hydrolysis of benzotrichloride
fun_facts: Benzoic acid was the first organic acid isolated from a natural source (benzoin resin, 16th century)
Widely used as a preservative in beverages and food products

c6h5nh2.name: Aniline
c6h5nh2.appearance: Colorless oily liquid, darkens on exposure to air
c6h5nh2.industrial: Production of dyes, polyurethanes, pharmaceuticals, rubber
fun_facts: The Zinin reaction (1842) — the first industrial synthesis of aniline — laid the foundation for the aniline dye industry
The name comes from the Portuguese 'anil' — indigo

c6h5oh.name: Phenol
c6h5oh.appearance: Colorless crystals with a characteristic odor (turn pink on exposure to air)
c6h5oh.industrial: Production of phenol-formaldehyde resins, bisphenol A, caprolactam
c6h5oh.production: Cumene process (from benzene and propylene — simultaneously with acetone)
fun_facts: The first antiseptic used in surgery (Joseph Lister, 1865)
Carbolic acid (phenol solution) was used to sterilize operating rooms

c8h18.name: Octane
fun_facts: Isooctane (2,2,4-trimethylpentane) is the reference standard for an octane rating of 100
The term 'octane number' in gasoline is derived from octane

ca_oh_2.name: Calcium hydroxide
ca_oh_2.production: Slaking quicklime with water: CaO + H₂O → Ca(OH)₂
fun_facts: Limewater (Ca(OH)₂ solution) is a qualitative reagent for CO₂: it turns cloudy when carbon dioxide is passed through it
Slaked lime has been used in construction for over 5,000 years

caco3.name: Calcium carbonate
caco3.production: Mining of natural minerals (limestone, chalk, marble)
fun_facts: Chalk, marble, limestone, seashells, and eggshells are all CaCO₃
Decomposes into CaO and CO₂ when heated — the basis of lime production

cao.name: Calcium oxide
hazards: Causes burns on contact with moist skin
cao.storage: Store in sealed containers, protect from moisture
cao.production: Calcination of limestone at 900–1200 °C: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂
fun_facts: Quicklime heats up intensely on contact with water — the reaction is highly exothermic
One of the oldest binding materials used in construction

ch3cho.name: Acetaldehyde
ch3cho.appearance: Colorless volatile liquid with a sharp fruity odor
ch3cho.industrial: Production of acetic acid, ethyl acetate, perfumery
ch3cho.production: Oxidation of ethanol, Wacker process (oxidation of ethylene)
fun_facts: Formed in the body during alcohol metabolism — acetaldehyde is what causes hangovers
Boils at 20°C — almost at room temperature

ch3coch3.name: Acetone
ch3coch3.appearance: Colorless volatile liquid with a characteristic odor
ch3coch3.industrial: Solvent (varnishes, paints, adhesives), production of methyl methacrylate, bisphenol A
ch3coch3.production: Cumene process (from benzene and propylene), dehydrogenation of isopropanol
fun_facts: Acetone is the most widely used solvent in the world
Produced in the body during fasting — the 'acetone breath' in diabetes

ch3cooc2h5.name: Ethyl acetate
ch3cooc2h5.appearance: Colorless volatile liquid with a pleasant fruity odor
ch3cooc2h5.industrial: Solvent (varnishes, adhesives, paints), food industry (flavoring agent)
ch3cooc2h5.production: Esterification of acetic acid with ethanol (Fischer esterification)
fun_facts: Ethyl acetate gives the characteristic smell to glue and nail polish remover
Food additive E1504 — used as a flavoring agent

ch3cooh.name: Acetic acid
ch3cooh.appearance: Colorless liquid with a sharp sour odor
ch3cooh.industrial: Production of acetate fibers, solvents, vinegar, vinyl acetate
ch3cooh.production: Oxidation of acetaldehyde, fermentation of ethanol (acetic acid fermentation)
fun_facts: Glacial acetic acid (100%) freezes at 16.6°C — hence the name 'glacial'
Table vinegar is a 6–9% solution of acetic acid in water

ch3coona.name: Sodium acetate
ch3coona.industrial: Food industry (E262), buffer solutions, chemical hand warmers
ch3coona.production: Neutralization of acetic acid with baking soda or NaOH
fun_facts: The basis of popular chemical hand warmers — crystallization of a supersaturated solution releases heat
Its aqueous solution is slightly alkaline (hydrolysis of the anion)

ch3oh.name: Methanol
ch3oh.appearance: Colorless liquid with a faint odor
ch3oh.production: Catalytic synthesis from CO and H₂ (synthesis gas)
fun_facts: Methanol is the simplest alcohol and one of the most toxic household substances
Used as fuel in Indianapolis 500 racing cars

ch4.name: Methane
ch4.industrial: Fuel (natural gas), production of hydrogen, carbon black, acetylene
ch4.production: Main component of natural gas, biogas (decomposition of organic matter)
fun_facts: Methane is the second most significant greenhouse gas after CO₂
A single cow produces up to 500 liters of methane per day

cl2.name: Chlorine
cl2.appearance: Yellowish-green gas with a sharp suffocating odor
fun_facts: The first poison gas used in World War I
Used to purify 98% of the world's drinking water

co2.name: Carbon dioxide
co2.industrial: Carbonation of beverages, fire extinguishing, soda production
co2.production: Byproduct of fuel combustion, alcoholic fermentation, and limestone calcination
fun_facts: Dry ice is solid CO₂; it sublimes at −78.5 °C, bypassing the liquid phase
CO₂ makes up about 0.04% of Earth's atmosphere, yet it is a key greenhouse gas

cu_oh_2.name: Copper(II) hydroxide
cu_oh_2.industrial: Production of fungicides (Bordeaux mixture), reagent in analytical chemistry
cu_oh_2.production: Precipitation from copper(II) salt solutions using alkali
fun_facts: Turns black when heated, decomposing into CuO and water
Dissolves in ammonia to form a bright blue solution (Schweizer's reagent)

cuo.name: Copper(II) oxide
cuo.industrial: Production of ceramics, pigments, and catalysts
cuo.production: Calcination of copper(II) hydroxide or heating copper in air
fun_facts: Easily reduced by hydrogen to metallic copper — a classic school chemistry experiment

cuso4.name: Copper(II) sulfate
cuso4.production: Dissolving copper in dilute sulfuric acid with heating and air exposure
fun_facts: The pentahydrate CuSO₄·5H₂O (blue vitriol) is bright blue; it loses water on heating and turns white
Anhydrous CuSO₄ is used as a moisture indicator — it turns blue on contact with water

fe2o3.name: Iron(III) oxide
fe2o3.industrial: Metallurgy (ore for pig iron production), pigments, polishing powders
fe2o3.production: Roasting of pyrite (FeS₂) or calcination of iron(III) hydroxide
fun_facts: The mineral hematite (Fe₂O₃) is the main iron ore on Earth
The red color of Martian soil is caused by the presence of Fe₂O₃

fe_oh_3.name: Iron(III) hydroxide
fe_oh_3.production: Precipitation from iron(III) salt solutions using alkali
fun_facts: Has no melting point — decomposes into Fe₂O₃ and water when heated
Rust is a mixture of hydrated iron oxides with a composition close to Fe(OH)₃

fecl2.name: Iron(II) chloride
fun_facts: Gradually oxidizes to FeCl₃ in air

fecl3.name: Iron(III) chloride
fecl3.appearance: Dark brown crystals, deliquescent in air
fecl3.industrial: Wastewater treatment, printed circuit board etching, catalyst
fecl3.production: Reaction of iron with chlorine gas at elevated temperature
fun_facts: FeCl₃ solution is a qualitative reagent for phenols (produces a violet coloration)

feo.name: Iron(II) oxide
feo.production: Reduction of Fe₂O₃ or thermal decomposition of iron(II) oxalate
fun_facts: Found in nature as the mineral wustite
Used as a black pigment in ceramics

feso4.name: Iron(II) sulfate
feso4.industrial: Water purification, ink production, agriculture (micronutrient fertilizer)
feso4.production: Dissolving iron in dilute sulfuric acid
fun_facts: The heptahydrate FeSO₄·7H₂O is known as green vitriol — one of the oldest known chemical compounds

h2.name: Hydrogen
h2.appearance: Colorless gas with no odor or taste
h2.industrial: Ammonia synthesis, fat hydrogenation, rocket fuel, fuel cells
h2.production: Steam reforming of methane, electrolysis of water
fun_facts: The lightest and most abundant element in the universe
A mixture of H₂ with air (4–75%) is explosive — known as 'detonating gas'

h2co3.name: Carbonic acid
h2co3.appearance: Exists only in aqueous solution; colorless liquid
h2co3.industrial: Carbonated beverages contain dissolved CO₂, which forms carbonic acid
h2co3.production: Dissolving CO₂ in water: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃
fun_facts: One of the most unstable acids — decomposes into water and CO₂ almost instantly
The fizzing of carbonated drinks is CO₂ being released as carbonic acid decomposes

h2o.name: Water
h2o.industrial: Universal solvent used in all branches of industry
fun_facts: The only substance on Earth found naturally in all three states of matter
Ice is less dense than liquid water — an anomalous property without which aquatic life in winter would be impossible
The water molecule has a bent geometry with a bond angle of 104.5°

h2o2.name: Hydrogen peroxide
h2o2.industrial: Bleaching, disinfection, rocket propellant, epoxide production
fun_facts: The 3% solution is the familiar 'hydrogen peroxide' found in first-aid kits
Bombardier beetles use H₂O₂ for a defensive 'explosion' at temperatures of ~100°C

h2s.name: Hydrogen sulfide
h2s.appearance: Colorless gas with a rotten egg smell
h2s.industrial: Sulfur production, analytical chemistry (precipitation of sulfides)
h2s.production: Reaction of sulfides with acids, byproduct of petroleum refining
fun_facts: The lethal concentration is just 0.1% in air
At high concentrations it paralyzes the sense of smell — the odor can no longer be detected

h2so4.name: Sulfuric acid
h2so4.storage: Store in glass or polyethylene containers, away from alkalis
h2so4.industrial: Production of fertilizers, batteries, and detergents
h2so4.production: Contact process: sulfur combustion → SO₂ → SO₃ → H₂SO₄
fun_facts: Sulfuric acid is called the 'bread of chemistry' — it is used in nearly all chemical manufacturing
When diluting, always add acid to water, never the reverse — otherwise violent boiling occurs

h3po4.name: Phosphoric acid
h3po4.industrial: Production of mineral fertilizers, food additive (E338), anti-corrosion treatment
h3po4.production: Extraction or thermal processing of apatites and phosphorites
fun_facts: Found in Coca-Cola and other soft drinks as an acidity regulator (E338)
Phosphoric acid is triprotic — it can form three series of salts

hcl.name: Hydrochloric acid
hcl.appearance: Colorless liquid with a pungent odor (in solution)
hcl.storage: Store in glass or polyethylene containers in a well-ventilated area
hcl.industrial: Metal cleaning, chloride production, food industry
hcl.production: Synthesis from hydrogen and chlorine, or as a byproduct of organic chlorination
fun_facts: Hydrochloric acid is present in human gastric juice (pH ≈ 1.5–2)
Concentrated HCl 'fumes' in air due to the release of gaseous HCl

hclo.name: Hypochlorous acid
hclo.appearance: Exists only in solution, pale yellow in color
hclo.production: Dissolving chlorine in water: Cl₂ + H₂O → HClO + HCl
fun_facts: The weakest of the chlorine-containing acids
HClO is what actually disinfects water during chlorination

hclo3.name: Chloric acid
hclo3.appearance: Exists only in solution, colorless liquid
hclo3.production: Reaction of Ba(ClO₃)₂ with sulfuric acid
fun_facts: Chlorates (salts of HClO₃) are used in match and firework production
In its free form it is unstable — decomposes into ClO₂ and HClO₄

hclo4.name: Perchloric acid
hazards: Explosive on contact with organic matter
hclo4.industrial: Analytical chemistry, production of perchlorates (rocket propellant)
hclo4.production: Reaction of NaClO₄ with HCl or electrochemical oxidation
fun_facts: The strongest of the chlorine-containing acids
Ammonium perchlorate NH₄ClO₄ is the main component of solid rocket fuel

hno3.name: Nitric acid
hno3.appearance: Colorless liquid with a pungent odor; fumes in air
hno3.storage: Store in dark glass containers, away from organic substances
hno3.industrial: Production of fertilizers (ammonium nitrate), explosives, and dyes
hno3.production: Catalytic oxidation of ammonia over a platinum catalyst (Ostwald process)
fun_facts: A mixture of HNO₃ and HCl (1:3) is 'aqua regia', which dissolves gold and platinum
Nitric acid turns skin yellow (xanthoproteic reaction with proteins)

k2so4.name: Potassium sulfate
k2so4.production: Reaction of KCl with sulfuric acid
fun_facts: One of the oldest mineral fertilizers
Unlike KCl, it contains no chlorine — preferred for chlorine-sensitive crops (tobacco, grapes, potatoes)

kcl.name: Potassium chloride
kcl.industrial: Production of potash fertilizers, medicine (salt substitute)
kcl.production: Mining of natural minerals sylvite and carnallite
fun_facts: The mineral sylvite (KCl) is the primary source of potash fertilizers
Used as a table salt substitute in dietary nutrition

kmno4.name: Potassium permanganate
kmno4.appearance: Dark purple crystals with a metallic luster
kmno4.production: Oxidation of manganates in alkaline medium
fun_facts: Known colloquially in Russia as 'margantsovka'
Its aqueous solution turns a vivid purple even at very low concentrations

kno3.name: Potassium nitrate
fun_facts: Saltpeter is one of the three components of black powder (along with sulfur and charcoal)
Used in the food industry for curing meat

koh.name: Potassium hydroxide
koh.storage: Store in sealed containers, protect from moisture and CO₂
koh.industrial: Production of soft soap, electrolytes for alkaline batteries
fun_facts: KOH is called 'caustic potash' — it readily destroys organic tissue
Absorbs CO₂ from the air, converting to potassium carbonate

mg_oh_2.name: Magnesium hydroxide
mg_oh_2.production: Precipitation from magnesium salt solutions using alkali
fun_facts: Mg(OH)₂ is the active ingredient in 'Milk of Magnesia', used to treat heartburn
Decomposes into MgO and water when heated

mgcl2.name: Magnesium chloride
mgcl2.industrial: Magnesium production (molten salt electrolysis), de-icing agent
mgcl2.production: Dissolving MgO or Mg(OH)₂ in hydrochloric acid, evaporation of seawater
fun_facts: The primary source of magnesium from seawater
Used as a de-icing agent instead of NaCl

mgo.name: Magnesium oxide
mgo.production: Calcination of magnesite MgCO₃ or dolomite
fun_facts: The melting point of MgO (2852°C) is one of the highest among oxides
Calcined magnesia is used by athletes to dry their hands

mncl2.name: Manganese(II) chloride
mncl2.production: Dissolving manganese or MnO₂ in hydrochloric acid
fun_facts: Its solution has the characteristic pale pink color of Mn²⁺ ions

mno2.name: Manganese(IV) oxide
mno2.industrial: Battery production, catalyst for H₂O₂ decomposition, pigment
mno2.production: Found in nature as the mineral pyrolusite
fun_facts: Has been used in batteries since the 19th century
Catalyzes the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide — a classic school experiment

n2.name: Nitrogen
n2.industrial: Ammonia production, inert atmosphere, food freezing
fun_facts: Makes up 78% of Earth's atmosphere
The triple bond N≡N is one of the strongest chemical bonds

na2co3.name: Sodium carbonate
na2co3.industrial: Production of glass, soap, detergents, and cellulose
na2co3.production: Solvay process (ammonia soda process) from NaCl and CaCO₃
fun_facts: Soda ash (Na₂CO₃) is one of the most important products of the chemical industry
The decahydrate Na₂CO₃·10H₂O is known as 'washing soda'

na2o.name: Sodium oxide
na2o.storage: Store in sealed containers, protect from moisture
na2o.industrial: Component of glass batches, intermediate in NaOH production
na2o.production: Heating sodium with limited oxygen supply
fun_facts: Rapidly absorbs moisture and CO₂ from air, converting to Na₂CO₃

na2so4.name: Sodium sulfate
na2so4.industrial: Production of glass, detergents, and cellulose
na2so4.production: Reaction of sulfuric acid with sodium chloride, or from natural minerals
fun_facts: The decahydrate Na₂SO₄·10H₂O (Glauber's salt) was discovered by Glauber in 1625 and was used as a laxative

naalo2.name: Sodium aluminate
naalo2.production: Dissolving Al₂O₃ or Al(OH)₃ in NaOH solution
fun_facts: Formed when aluminium dissolves in alkali — a typical reaction of an amphoteric metal
Used in construction as a concrete setting accelerator

nacl.name: Sodium chloride
nacl.industrial: Food industry, soda and chlorine production (electrolysis)
nacl.production: Mining of rock salt or evaporation of seawater
fun_facts: Annual global consumption is about 300 million tonnes
In Ancient Rome, soldiers were sometimes paid in salt — hence the word 'salary'
NaCl crystals have a cubic shape, visible even in ordinary table salt

naoh.name: Sodium hydroxide
naoh.storage: Store in sealed containers, protect from moisture
naoh.industrial: Production of soap, paper, viscose; petroleum product refining
naoh.production: Electrolysis of sodium chloride solution (chloralkali process)
fun_facts: NaOH is called 'caustic soda' or 'lye'
Absorbs CO₂ and moisture from air, gradually converting to Na₂CO₃

nh3.name: Ammonia
nh3.appearance: Colorless gas with a pungent odor
nh3.storage: Store in steel cylinders in a well-ventilated area
nh3.industrial: Production of nitrogen fertilizers, nitric acid, and explosives
nh3.production: Synthesis from nitrogen and hydrogen at high pressure and temperature (Haber process)
fun_facts: Smelling salts (ammonia solution) are used to revive people who have fainted
Ammonia is one of the most widely produced chemicals in the world (over 150 million tonnes/year)

nh4cl.name: Ammonium chloride
nh4cl.production: Reaction of ammonia with hydrochloric acid, byproduct of the Solvay process
fun_facts: Known since antiquity as 'sal ammoniac' — named after the Temple of Ammon in Egypt
Sublimes when heated, bypassing the liquid phase

o2.name: Oxygen
o2.production: Fractional distillation of liquid air or electrolysis of water
fun_facts: Makes up 21% of Earth's atmosphere
Liquid oxygen is blue in color and is attracted by magnets

pb_no3_2.name: Lead(II) nitrate
pb_no3_2.storage: Store in sealed containers, away from combustible materials, in a ventilated area
pb_no3_2.industrial: Production of pigments, flotation reagents, pyrotechnics
pb_no3_2.production: Dissolving lead or lead oxide in nitric acid
fun_facts: One of the few soluble lead salts — most Pb²⁺ salts are insoluble
Decomposes when heated, releasing brown NO₂ gas

sio2.name: Silicon dioxide
sio2.industrial: Production of glass, ceramics, optical fiber, and electronics
sio2.production: Mining of natural quartz sand; purification for electronics
fun_facts: Quartz, rock crystal, amethyst, and agate are natural forms of SiO₂
SiO₂ is the basis of silicon chips that power all modern electronics
The most abundant mineral in the Earth's crust

so2.name: Sulfur dioxide
so2.appearance: Colorless gas with a sharp suffocating odor
so2.production: Roasting of sulfur or sulfide ores
fun_facts: One of the main atmospheric pollutants that causes acid rain
Has been used as a preservative in winemaking for over 2,000 years

so3.name: Sulfur trioxide
so3.storage: Store in sealed containers, protect from moisture
so3.industrial: Intermediate product in the manufacture of sulfuric acid
so3.production: Catalytic oxidation of SO₂ with atmospheric oxygen (contact process)
fun_facts: SO₃ avidly absorbs water — when released into the atmosphere it forms sulfuric acid (acid rain)
A solution of SO₃ in sulfuric acid is called oleum

v2o5.name: Vanadium(V) oxide
v2o5.industrial: Catalyst in the contact process (H₂SO₄ production), alloy manufacturing
fun_facts: The most important industrial catalyst — sulfuric acid production via the contact process is impossible without it

zno.name: Zinc oxide
zno.appearance: White powder; turns yellow when heated
zno.industrial: Rubber production (vulcanization), zinc white pigment, cosmetics, pharmaceuticals
zno.production: Oxidation of zinc vapor with atmospheric oxygen
fun_facts: Found in zinc ointment — an antiseptic skin treatment
Turns yellow when heated and white again when cooled — a thermochromic effect
